summary: Save/restore pending throw state across unwind-protect cleanup so a nested unwind-protect's OP_UWPOP can't clobber an in-progress non-local transfer.

items.title: Save and restore pending throw state across UWP cleanup
items.description:
Bug: when an unwind-protect cleanup runs during an in-progress non-local transfer (cl_pending_throw == 1 from invoke-restart/throw, or == 2 from error), a nested unwind-protect inside the cleanup completing normally executes OP_UWPOP which clears cl_pending_throw to 0. The outer UWP's OP_UWRETHROW then sees no pending transfer and the abort handler is never reached. This is the same family as the 0016 multiple-value leak — a nested UWP frame clobbers shared transfer state. Fix: give every unwind-protect frame a private snapshot of cl_pending_* so its execution can't bleed into surrounding frames.

Implementation (all per-thread):

1) src/core/thread.h
   - Define CL_SavedPending { int pending_throw; CL_Obj pending_tag; CL_Obj pending_value; int pending_mv_count; CL_Obj pending_mv_values[CL_MAX_MV]; int pending_error_code; char pending_error_msg[512]; } and CL_MAX_SAVED_PENDING (suggest 256 main, matches NLX depth tolerance — pushed once per active UWP arming).
   - Add to CL_Thread_s: heap-allocated `CL_SavedPending *saved_pending_stack;` `int saved_pending_top;` `int saved_pending_max;` (heap-allocate same as nlx_stack to avoid bloating the struct — at ~620 bytes/entry the static array is too big).
   - Add access macros: cl_saved_pending_stack, cl_saved_pending_top, cl_saved_pending_max.

2) src/core/thread.c
   - In cl_main_thread_init: allocate saved_pending_stack with CL_MAX_SAVED_PENDING entries via platform_alloc.
   - In worker thread init (cl_thread_alloc): allocate with a smaller bound (e.g., 64).
   - Free in cl_main_thread_shutdown / cl_thread_free.
   - Zero saved_pending_top at init.

3) src/core/vm.h
   - Add `int saved_pending_mark;` to CL_NLXFrame (just one int — keep the per-frame overhead minimal).

4) src/core/vm.c — at every NLX-frame setup site, capture saved_pending_mark; at every longjmp landing, restore cl_saved_pending_top to that mark.
   a) OP_CATCH (line 3199): set `nlx->saved_pending_mark = cl_saved_pending_top;` before setjmp. In the longjmp branch, add `cl_saved_pending_top = nlx->saved_pending_mark;` alongside the other mark restores.
   b) OP_BLOCK_PUSH (line 2631): same — capture before setjmp, restore in longjmp branch.
   c) OP_TAGBODY_PUSH (line 2754, full body not shown above but follows the same pattern): same.
   d) OP_UWPROT (line 3280): in the setjmp==0 (normal arming) branch, BEFORE incrementing cl_nlx_top, push the current pending state into saved_pending_stack and clear cl_pending_throw to 0 (and zero pending_tag/value/mv_count/error_code/error_msg for cleanliness). Capture `nlx->saved_pending_mark = cl_saved_pending_top` AFTER the push so the mark equals the post-push depth (so a longjmp landing in our own setjmp branch does not undo our push). In the longjmp branch (line 3322), restore `cl_saved_pending_top = nlx->saved_pending_mark;` alongside the other mark restores. Overflow check: if cl_saved_pending_top >= cl_saved_pending_max → cl_error(CL_ERR_OVERFLOW, "saved-pending overflow").
   e) OP_UWPOP (line 3390): REMOVE the `cl_pending_throw = 0;` line on line 3405 — clearing it here is what truncates the outer transfer; we no longer need it because UWPROT setup already cleared pt for the body.
   f) OP_UWRETHROW (line 3409): at the top of the case, pop one saved entry: `CL_SavedPending saved = cl_saved_pending_stack[--cl_saved_pending_top];` (the pop is unconditional since OP_UWPROT always pushes). Then: if cl_pending_throw != 0 → execute the existing re-throw logic unchanged (the popped `saved` is discarded; any longjmp out of this case is fine — the next frame's landing will restore saved_pending_top via its mark). Else (cl_pending_throw == 0) → restore cl_pending_* from `saved` and fall through (no re-throw at this level — the outer UWP's OP_UWRETHROW will see the restored state).

5) src/core/error.c — cl_error_unwind and the CL_ERR_EXIT paths reset cl_nlx_top to 0; mirror that by also resetting `cl_saved_pending_top = 0;` so saved entries don't leak across error boundaries.

6) Other NLX-mark resets — check builtins_thread.c (thread-exit cleanup that resets nlx_top), debugger.c, and any other site that resets cl_nlx_top to 0 outside the VM loop; reset cl_saved_pending_top alongside. Pattern: grep for `cl_nlx_top = 0` and `t->nlx_top = 0`.

7) cl_throw_to_tag (builtins_condition.c line 1159), block/tagbody throws in vm.c (lines 2722, 2840), error throws (error.c line 64) — NO change needed: they all longjmp to the destination NLX frame, whose landing handler does the saved_pending_top restore via the captured mark. The `cl_nlx_top = j` (pop the destination frame) behavior is preserved unchanged.

8) Tests:
   a) tests/test_condition.c — add regression tests:
      - The exact 18-line pure-CL repro from the request: (restart-case (unwind-protect (invoke-restart 'abort) (nested-uwp ...)) (abort () ...)) must hit the handler.
      - Two-deep nested UWP in cleanup: (restart-case (unwind-protect X (unwind-protect nil (unwind-protect nil nil))) (abort () :ok)).
      - Nested UWP in cleanup that itself throws (cleanup does (catch 'inner (unwind-protect (throw 'inner :x) nil))) — outer handler must still fire.
      - Error path equivalent: error in body + nested UWP in cleanup body completing normally — error must still propagate.
      - Confirm the existing nested-uwp-cleanup-order test (line 911) still passes (its UWP nesting is on the body side, not the cleanup side, but it's a useful regression anchor).
   b) tests/amiga/run-tests.lisp — add the same restart/nested-uwp repro as a Lisp-level test that must report handler-ran = T.

9) make test (host) must pass; make -f Makefile.cross test-amiga must pass.

No FASL version bump needed: bytecode emission is unchanged (no new opcodes, no operand encoding changes — only the runtime semantics of the existing OP_UWPROT/UWPOP/UWRETHROW change).
